blocks.text: Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Jeffrey Perez has accepted the position of Design Technician and joins the Engineering group. He comes in at the quiet end of the year, which suits me. There is time to put him through the standards and the detail library before the spring work arrives, and I intend to use all of it.

His work will be details and quantity takeoffs, with plan production as the balance of it. I want to be direct with him about why that assignment matters more than the title suggests. A takeoff is the number a contractor prices. A quantity that is wrong is not a drafting error somebody catches on the check print; it is a bid error, and it comes back to this office months later wearing a different name and asking for money. Jeffrey Perez has no wish to be the reason that happens. That disposition is most of the reason he is sitting here, and I would rather have it than speed.

For his first weeks he will work against sets that have already been issued rather than live ones. He is to take off quantities from those sheets, compare his numbers against what was tabulated at the time, and bring me every difference, including the ones he believes are his own error. Especially those. Where a detail does not make sense to him he is to ask what it is for, not merely what it ought to look like. I am told he asks a great many questions. He will find that welcome here and he should not moderate it on my account, though I would ask that he pick his moment when an engineer is on the telephone with a reviewer.

The ordinary matters, briefly. Time is booked to a job number the day it is worked. Drawing files live in the lettered project folder on the share and are not kept on a local drive. Anything that leaves this office does so over my signature and after my review, which is not a remark on Jeffrey Perez but the standing rule and applies to work of mine as readily as to his. We are glad to have him.
